title: Uniface and 3GL
text: Uniface and 3GL When you build a Uniface application, you might occasionally find that some tasks cannot be done optimally, or even not at all, in ProcScript. You might also find that the Uniface application needs to use legacy code that is available only in 3GL, or it might be required to embed Uniface components in a new or existing 3GL application. You might want to use 3GL code to do one or more of the following: Execute 3GL code from a Uniface application (call-out). A typical scenario is when you need to perform complex arithmetic calculations, such as scientific or statistical functions. Execute Uniface code from a 3GL application (call-in) Access data in a DBMS for which no Uniface connector is available (user-defined database connectors) Handle data in a format that Uniface does not understand (user-defined packing codes). Implement data encryption and decryption using the formatToDbms and formatFromDbms triggers (user-defined data encryption) Define your own GUI components (user-defined widgets). Provide custom server logon validation and/or network data encryption (security driver). Customize the handling of asynchronous messages (asynchronous message handlers). See uasync . Before deciding if and how to use 3GL, you should ask yourself whether 3GL is the right solution for your problem. The Uniface ProcScript language is very flexible and can do more things than perhaps you might have thought possible. On the other hand, ProcScript cannot do some things that 3GL languages excel at, such as working with pointers or arrays. For CPU-intensive computations, it might be better to use 3GL, even though you could do it in 4GL, because native code generated by a 3GL compiler usually performs better than the intermediate code generated by the Uniface compiler. Related Links Call-Out to 3GL You can call out to 3GL functions from ProcScript using the activate ProcSCript statement. The 3GL functions called could be your own functions, functions in existing libraries (legacy code), or even functions in operating system libraries. Call-In From 3GL Call-in from 3GL is the Uniface terminology for executing Uniface components from 3GL code, which could be either a stand-alone 3GL program, or a 3GL function that is called from Uniface with activate. Database Connector Interface The Database Connector Interface is an API for building custom database connectors. Security Drivers and the Security Driver API APIs: 3GL Functions Unless otherwise indicated, all 3GL functions are available on all platforms. 3GL Development Issues Issues that can affect your Uniface and 3GL development include licensing, multi-threading, signal handling and C++ name mangling.
